title: Zelensky Ousts Army Chief After Protests, Turkey's Opposition Splits, US Seeks $1.5 Trillion War Budget
subhead: Ukraine's political crisis deepens as the popular defence minister's firing sparks protests that force out the top general; Turkey's opposition fractures as Ozel announces a new party; and Hegseth reveals the Iran war has cost $37.5 billion so far as the Pentagon seeks a record budget.

sections.heading: Zelensky Sacks Army Commander After Protests Force His Hand
sections.body:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky dismissed army commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi on July 21, replacing him with 43-year-old Major General Mykhailo Drapatyi, a popular battle-hardened commander seen as more innovative. The move came after six days of street protests across Ukraine demanding Syrskyi's removal, triggered by Zelensky's earlier firing of Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov — a 35-year-old reformer widely credited with modernising the armed forces and fighting corruption.

The crisis began when Fedorov was replaced by Yevhenii Khmara, a former top official at Ukraine's Security Service (SBU). Fedorov and Syrskyi had clashed over military strategy and reforms, with Syrskyi viewed by critics as a holdover from Soviet-era thinking. Fedorov publicly endorsed Drapatyi's appointment, calling it 'a new hope in the fight for freedom and justice' and 'the voice of change that could not be ignored.' Zelensky also offered Fedorov a 'worthy position in power,' though it remains unclear whether he accepted.

The personnel upheaval comes as Ukraine faces mounting challenges: violent attacks on military recruitment officers have exceeded 600 incidents this year, and Russia continues to press its advantage on the front line. Zelensky has asked both Khmara and Drapatyi to develop a new defence strategy covering military corps reform, weapons supply, and drones.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine has effectively replaced both its civilian and military defence leadership in the span of a week, in the middle of an active war. Whether Drapatyi can deliver the faster, more innovative command structure Fedorov championed may determine Ukraine's ability to hold the line against Russia.

sections.heading: US-Iran War Enters 10th Night of Strikes as Costs Mount
sections.body:
The US launched its 10th consecutive night of strikes on Iran, with Central Command reporting hits on military command centres, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and air defence systems. Explosions were reported near Tabriz — where state media said one person was killed — and at the Gulf ports of Bandar Mahshahr, Bandar Imam Khomeini, Chabahar, and Konarak. President Trump said the strikes were 'in honour' of three US soldiers killed by Iranian attacks — two at Muwaffaq Salti base in Jordan and one in Iraq — and warned that Iran would 'pay.'

Iran responded by striking two oil tankers in the Strait of Hormuz, according to state media, with the UK Maritime Trade Operations agency confirming a tanker hit by an unknown projectile forced its crew to abandon ship. Iran's IRGC also claimed cruise missile strikes on an Amazon data centre in Bahrain, while Jordan intercepted five drones, and Kuwait reported Iranian attacks on power and desalination plants. President Pezeshkian declared Iran was fighting a 'full-scale war' with the US.

Despite the fighting, Tehran said diplomatic messages were still being exchanged through intermediaries. Trump insisted Iran 'desperately' wants to meet, but added the US had 'no interest' until Iran was 'ready to meet in a meaningful way.' He also threatened strikes on Mount Kolang Gaz La (Pickaxe Mountain), a site reportedly housing Iranian nuclear centrifuges.
sections.whyItMatters: The conflict has settled into a sustained exchange of strikes with no ceasefire in sight. Diplomatic backchannels remain technically open but have produced nothing, while Hormuz traffic has slowed to a trickle and Iran is expanding its targets to include Gulf state infrastructure and commercial data centres.

sections.heading: Pentagon Seeks $1.5 Trillion Budget as Iran War Hits $37.5 Billion
sections.body:
Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth revealed the US-Iran war has cost $37.5 billion so far, testifying before the Senate Appropriations Committee alongside Joint Chiefs Chairman Dan Caine in support of a $1.5 trillion defence budget that includes $70 billion for the Iran war. The figure is roughly $8 billion higher than the administration's last public estimate of $29 billion in May, though still well below independent estimates. Moody's Analytics has placed the domestic cost as high as $150 billion when accounting for higher energy prices and knock-on effects.

Hegseth acknowledged strains on the US weapons stockpile and said funding was needed 'to expand production lines and accelerate delivery of high-demand munitions,' including solid rocket motors, JDAMs, hypersonics, and counter-drone systems. He warned that without the budget, military training 'would have to be curtailed.' Democratic Senator Patty Murray called the sprawling request — which also includes National Guard deployment in DC, anti-drug operations in the Caribbean, and border patrols — poorly justified, saying 'the truth is, your request does not make a lot of sense.'
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran war is burning through resources faster than the administration has acknowledged. The gap between the official $37.5 billion figure and outside estimates up to $150 billion suggests the true cost — including economic spillovers — is being understated, and the weapons stockpile strain raises questions about US readiness for any additional confrontation.

sections.heading: Turkey's Opposition Splits as Ozel Announces New Party
sections.body:
Deposed Republican People's Party (CHP) leader Ozgur Ozel announced in parliament on July 21 that he is forming a new political party, two months after an appeals court stripped him of the CHP chairmanship and reinstated the unpopular former leader Kemal Kilicdaroglu. Ozel called the move 'the hope of a new beginning' and said the new party would become parliament's main opposition 'by a wide margin,' with many of the CHP's 135 legislators expected to defect.

The split comes amid a sweeping government crackdown on Turkey's opposition: hundreds of elected municipal officials and party members have been detained since late 2024, including Istanbul mayor Ekrem Imamoglu, who has been imprisoned since March 2025 and faces criminal cases carrying decades-long sentences. Ozel and critics call the court ruling against him and the broader crackdown a politically motivated 'coup,' while the government insists the courts are independent.

Analysts warn the split could benefit President Erdogan by dividing opposition forces. Murat Gezici of pollster Gezici said: 'In the short- and medium-term, the political arithmetic works in favour of Erdogan and the AK Party.' Ozel's supporters argue a unified new party would be more effective than the court-manipulated CHP, but the risk of a fractured opposition in a country where Erdogan has ruled for over two decades is significant.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's opposition is splitting at exactly the moment unity would matter most — hundreds of opposition figures are jailed or detained, and Erdogan has consolidated power for two decades. Whether Ozel's new party consolidates the opposition or fractures it further could determine whether Turkey has a viable democratic challenge in the next election cycle.

sections.heading: Trump Slaps 50% Tariffs on Canada as Carney Vows 'All Options' Response
sections.body:
President Trump imposed 50% tariffs on a wide range of Canadian imports — including wine, dairy, cement, furniture, hockey sticks, and clothing — invoking Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930, a provision never used in nearly a century. The tariffs, set to take effect in 30 days, target roughly $20 billion of the $382 billion in annual US imports from Canada. Energy, potash, critical minerals, and fish are exempt. The move came with three proclamations citing Canadian discrimination against US cars, dairy, and alcohol.

Prime Minister Mark Carney said he and Trump had agreed to 'intensify' trade negotiations but warned 'all options' were on the table for Canada's response. He framed the tariffs as the latest in 'a series of unilateral US trade actions in direct violation of the USMCA.' Ontario Premier Doug Ford called for Canada to respond 'tariff for tariff, dollar for dollar,' while BC Premier David Eby declared there was 'not a chance in hell' US alcohol returns to provincial shelves. US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer signalled broader action ahead, telling CNBC to 'expect to see some action soon' on tariffs for other countries.

The tariffs add to existing US duties of 15-50% on Canadian steel, aluminium, and copper, and a 25% tax on non-US car parts. Canada maintains 25% counter-tariffs on US steel, aluminium, and vehicles. The Canadian dollar dipped to a one-week low.
sections.whyItMatters: Trump is deploying a century-old tariff law never before used, targeting Canada's economy in what amounts to a direct assault on the USMCA trade framework he originally negotiated. Carney faces a choice between escalation and negotiation, while provincial premiers are already signalling unwillingness to compromise on retaliatory measures like alcohol bans.

sections.heading: Lebanese Army Deploys to Pilot Zone as Israel Fires Warning Shots
sections.body:
Lebanese army engineering units entered the village of Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh in the country's south on July 22 under a US-brokered trilateral framework, the first phase of a plan for Israeli withdrawal and Hezbollah disarmament. Israel said it would 'adjust its force posture' in one pilot area but warned it would remain in its 10km 'security zone' along the border 'as long as necessary.' The deployment coincided with Lebanese President Joseph Aoun's visit to the White House — the first by a Lebanese head of state in nearly 17 years — where Trump pledged help for Lebanon.

Almost immediately, the Lebanese army reported Israeli forces 'opened fire in the vicinity of these units,' while the IDF said it fired 'warning shots into the air' after Lebanese troops crossed about 150 metres into Israel's declared security zone. No injuries were reported. Hezbollah has rejected the agreement entirely, calling provisions linking Israeli withdrawal to disarmament a crossing of 'all red lines.'

More than 4,300 people have been killed in Lebanon since the conflict began, including almost 800 children, women, and medics, according to the Lebanese health ministry. Israel says 37 of its soldiers and four civilians have been killed on both sides of the border. The UN human rights office has condemned the recent intensification of Israeli attacks in Gaza.
sections.whyItMatters: The pilot zone is the first concrete test of the US-brokered Lebanon framework, and it immediately ran into trouble — warning shots within hours of deployment signal how fragile the arrangement is. With Hezbollah rejecting the deal outright and Israel refusing to set a withdrawal timeline, the 'pilot' could stall before it scales.

bottomLine: Ukraine's war leadership underwent a dramatic shakeup as public pressure forced Zelensky to replace his top general, while the Iran-US conflict ground through its 10th night of strikes with no ceasefire in sight and costs spiralling past $37.5 billion. Turkey's opposition fractured into two parties as Ozel launched a new movement that may inadvertently help Erdogan. Trump's 50% tariffs on Canada using a century-old legal provision escalated North American trade tensions to new heights. And in Lebanon, the first test of the US-brokered withdrawal framework ran into immediate trouble when Israeli forces fired warning shots at deploying Lebanese troops — a reminder that even the most carefully negotiated arrangements can collapse in seconds on the ground.
